title: Cream cheese frosting
subtitle: Tangy, lightly sweet frosting for carrot cake, red velvet, and spiced layers
excerpt: Cream cheese and butter beaten together with icing sugar and vanilla. The tang of the cheese balances rich sponges where a pure buttercream would be cloying. The frosting is softer than an all-butter buttercream and needs to be used at cool room temperature.
sourceNotes: Cream cheese as a cake frosting is documented in American domestic baking from the mid-twentieth century, appearing in standardised form alongside the popularisation of cream cheese in the 1950s and 1960s. Earlier British equivalents used soured cream or fresh curd cheese in similar ratios; the technique of combining a fresh dairy cheese with sugar as a cake topping is recorded in domestic household guides of the late nineteenth century. The basic method is now standard and public-domain across all English-language baking references.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough to fill and frost a 2-layer 20 cm cake
recipe.batchNotes: Scale directly. Keep the butter-to-cheese ratio constant.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead and refrigerate. Remove 30 minutes before using; beat briefly to loosen.

glossaryTerms.definition: Cream cheese frosting softens faster than all-butter buttercream because of the cheese's higher moisture content. For spreading on a stacked cake, it should be the consistency of thick Greek yoghurt at cool room temperature (around 18°C). Warmer than that and it will start to slip; refrigerate the frosted cake for 15 to 20 minutes between layers to keep the stack stable. Do not over-beat once the icing sugar is in: too much air causes the frosting to separate slightly and look curdled.

content.text: Cream cheese frosting has more moisture than buttercream, which gives it its characteristic silky texture but makes it softer at the same temperature. Work at cool room temperature and refrigerate the finished cake until an hour before serving.

Beat the softened {{unsalted-butter}} alone for 2 minutes until pale. Add the {{cream-cheese}} and beat on medium until just combined, about 1 minute. Do not over-beat at this stage; too much air causes a loose, slightly grainy texture.
Add the sifted {{icing-sugar}} in two additions on low speed, then add the {{vanilla-extract}}. Increase to medium and beat for 30 seconds. Check the
: it should hold its shape briefly when you lift the beater but drop within a second or two. If it is too soft, refrigerate for 10 minutes before using.

Spread between layers and over the cake with an icing spatula. For a smooth finish, use long, firm strokes and keep the spatula at a shallow angle. Refrigerate the frosted cake until 45 minutes before serving.
